topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne glasses seemed amplified in the cavernous ballroom, each sound a tiny needle pricking at my nerves. I kept my gaze fixed on the elaborate floral arrangements, tracing the patterns with my eyes as if the sheer volume of happy chatter could somehow absorb the ache in my chest. Then, there he was. Standing near the entrance, a familiar silhouette against the glittering backdrop, and the air seemed to thicken around me. My breath hitched, a shallow, sharp intake that did nothing to settle the churning in my stomach. He offered a tentative smile, and the practiced ease of his presence felt like a deliberate slight. I gripped my wine glass so tightly my knuckles turned white, the liquid inside suddenly tasting metallic and bitter. I made a swift, deliberate pivot towards the buffet table, needing the immediate, solid reality of food to anchor me against the sudden, unwelcome collision of the past and the present.

[story 2]

I was trying to look engaged in the conversation about the caterer’s choices, nodding vaguely when Sarah spoke about the appetizers, but my attention kept snagging on the doorway. It was him. Unannounced, unexpected. A sudden, unwelcome tightening started low in my abdomen, a dull, persistent pressure that made my shoulders tense up beneath my silk dress. When our eyes met across the room, the easy camaraderie that had existed between us years ago evaporated, replaced by a thick, awkward silence that felt deafening. I forced a bright, brittle laugh when he finally approached, the sound feeling foreign and strained in my own ears. "Well, look who it is," I managed, the words clipped and too loud. I immediately turned my body away, focusing intently on a distant painting, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

Every time he laughed at something another guest said, a distinct, unpleasant clenching occurred in my jaw. I watched him from across the dance floor, cataloging every casual gesture, every relaxed posture, and it only served to amplify the sense of distance between us. It felt like standing on a tightrope, acutely aware of the drop below. When he finally navigated through the crowd and stopped near my group, the entire atmosphere around him seemed to shift—a subtle, undeniable disruption. I felt a reflexive urge to retreat, to physically shrink into myself. My hands started fiddling with the strap of my clutch bag, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. When he spoke, his tone was too familiar, too warm, and I found myself nodding stiffly, my expression carefully blank, while my internal monologue was screaming a desperate plea for the moment to simply cease existing.
